                Business                         Information                              Systems
                 Issues                             Issues                                 Issues

What   ar e  t oday’ s  obj ect i ves?


Deepen our knowledge of systems          through systems thinking


Build an understanding of business processes, levels and categories


Explain business process management (BPM), its role and value


Apply the basic symbols of BPMN (business process modeling notation), understand the value


Explain the three primary organization levels and the management information needed for
decisions at each level

Systems Thinking
Parts | Bounds | Relationships

What   i s  syst ems  t hi nki ng?


It’s making sense of a complex situation or thing by breaking it down
into parts and relations


It’s been around for a long time and is a key tool underpinning                    all
information systems work –           and a whole lot of other fields including
engineering and most of the sciences


We   c a n   u s e   i t   t o   h e l p   u s  understand a situation, design a new
approach, or for ongoing monitoring


In business information systems we use it to understand businesses,
the contexts they exist in, and to design the IT systems that support
those business –      including the people, the technology, and the
processes










See: Magnus Ramage and Karen Shipp. 2009. Systems Thinkers. Springer.

What   t he  t he  basi cs?                                                                                    Outside System


1.  Define the boundary         of the system (e.g., What is inside? What                                       Inside System
    is outside?... it is up to you!)


2.  Define the objects, parts        or things    inside the system (e.g., Are                            Part 1
    they People? Organisational           Units? Computers? Activites?                                                          Part 3
    Customers … it ’s up to you!)

3.  Show how the parts are re l at e d  (e.g., Are they related by                                    Relation 1
    flows of information? Social relationships? Organisational                                                        Part 2
    re l at i ons hi p s ?   R e p or t i ng   l i ne s ? )   …  i t   i s   up   t o  y ou! )


                                                                                                                               Relation 2
 With this basic way of thinking about problems, we can unpack
      and simplify many problems in business … like business
                                    processes

Business Processes
What | Why | Challenges | Role of IS…

What   i s  a  Busi ness  Pr ocess?


A business process     is a collection of linked tasks which
find their end in the delivery of a service or product to
a client


Thinking in terms of processes instead of tasks allows
us to have a more holistic view of the business


We   c a n  focus on flows of information, resources, and
outputs, not just individual organisation units

What   about   an  exampl e?


Let ’s think about Swinburne University…

          Student application processing                 …and outputs from one
                                                          process can become
                                                            inputs to another

          Class registration


          Academic performance reporting


          Payroll


          Accounts payable


          Inventory management


          Sales


          Procurement

Why  i s  ‘ pr ocess’   t hi nki ng  i mpor t ant ?


Previously, we understood business via organisational charts, functions, and individual unit roles
and responsibilities


This made sense in the paper-based          paradigm


In the digital age, thinking in terms of processes helps understand the business in terms of flows
of information


This can help to rev e al  information bottlenecks, redundancies, and efficiency opportunities


Remember, data is now often the most valuable             asset a business has!

How do we categori se busi ness processes?

..in terms of scope of actors involved?

                                                                                                                                        of an organisation

How can we categori se busi ness processes? (cont.)


..or in terms of managerial task

                                                                               Strategic Processes
                                                                               •Strategic processes plan, coordinate,
                                                                                measure, monitor and control activities
                                                                                related to business performance.
                                                                                Examples of management processes
                                                                                include governance, strategic planning,
                                                                                budgeting, and infrastructure / capacity
                                                                                management.





                                                                                                              Management/ Reporting
                                                          Operational Processes                               Processes
                                                          •Operational processes                              •upporting  processes at an BU,
                                                           represent essential business                        cross-BU and  also project level,
                                                           activities that accomplish                          these can include performance
                                                           business objectives, e.g.,                          reporting, and activities
                                                           generating revenue.                                 associated with the planning,
                                                                                                               coordination and governance
                                                                                                               of business and project
                                                                                                               activities

What   ar e  key  busi ness  pr ocess  chal l enges?


Bottlenecks   – slowing or halting of a process at a specific stage due to a
human/machine error or difficulty


Duplication   of work and redundancy –       inefficient processes often have areas that
are repeated or unnecessary


Lack of visibility – management does not have a way of viewing where processes
stand, what their status is, and how they are performing generally, and specifically


Integration  – process technology does not always integrate well with existing
systems, causing communication gaps and malfunctions.


Contextually inappropriate      – new processes may not fit the environment they are
implementing into

What   i s  t he  r ol e  of  i nfor mat i on  syst ems?


Business processes predominantly happen within an Information System


Applications will   coordinate the sequential and concurrent activities associated with business
processes


Applications provide access      to data –  the ‘oil’ of business processes


Applications provide monitoring        and auditing capability across business processes


…and also re p or t i ng

Business are often
dependent on multiple
systems to interface
effectively in order to                                                          Cloud
properly execute cross-unit                                                                                           Inform ation
and interorganizational                                                                                                    •   Model
processes                                                                                   Data                           •   Report
                                                                                                                           •   Tr a  n  s  f   o  r  m
Most organisations                                                                                                         •   Measure
                                                                                                                           •   Store
would have 1000’s of
applications supporting
100’s processes

                                                                                                                         Knowledge

What   happens  when  i nfor mat i on  syst ems  don’ t
support business processes?

When systems do not ‘talk’ to each other then processes break down


Inefficiencies emerge

Manual entry                                                                                     ..hence,
                                                                                             IS experts have
                                                                                          developed tools and
Lookup tables / mapping tables                                                               techniques for
                                                                                           understanding and
                                                                                           improving business
Intermediary applications                                                                       processes


The portfolio of organizational applications grows exponentially

Modelling & Diagrams
Mapping | Modelling | BPM(S) | Swim Lanes

What   i s  busi ness  pr ocess  mappi ng?


Business process mapping            is describing a process diagrammatically








Click to edit Master text styles
It is also called modelling        or diagramming
Second level

   • Third level
     • Fourth level
       •  Fifth level

It includes…
            Start and end points,
            Inputs, outputs (generally data flows)
            The people/ organisational roles involved
            Decision points, triggers, controls (generally sign-offs)
            Concurrent processes
            ….

What   ar e  t he  st eps  for   cr eat i ng  a  pr ocess  di agr am?


1.  Process identification -     identify objectives, scope, players and work areas.
2.  Information gathering -       gather process facts (what, who, where, when) from the people who
    do the work.
3.  Select diagramming technique –          BPMN, ARIS, Swimlane, UML
4.  Process Mapping -      convert facts into a process map.
5.  Analysis -  work through the map, challenging each step (what-why ?, who-why ?, where-why ?,
    when-why ?, how-why ?)
6.  Remediation -     develop/Install new activities -      eliminate unnecessary work, combine steps,
    re ar rang e   s t e p s ,   ad d   new   s t e p s   w he re   ne ce s s ar y
7.  Monitor -   manage process -      maintain process map in library, review routinely, and monitor
    process for changes

                      If it is not correct the manager
                      returns the rep o rt to  the
                      consultant. If it is correct the
                      manager forwards the report to
                      the Data Entry Clerk who enters
                      the data into the system.

Whi ch  model l i ng  appr oach  shoul d  I   use?


Modelling is a core      skill of the IS specialist


In most IS Units you will learn some sort of new modelling technique


Many modelling languages            and tools    have developed over decades


Different organizations will use different            tools


Yo  u      n  e   e   d       t   o      b   e       a  b   l   e    to use a number of techniques –formal and informal


Rule: in professional settings use what suits the audience at a particular time

What   t ool s  hel p  manage  pr ocesses?

visibility and bottlenecks                                                               In high volum e autom ated

common ERP/CRM and other systems, preventing the need for                                limited application –     ‘horses for
error-prone coding                                                                       courses’


Collaboration Tools –    developers who can easily collaborate
and view each other  ’s work are able to help prevent
duplication of work and redundancy

Summary
Think like a process.
